data.title: Assessing First Merchants (FRME) Valuation After Recent Share Price Weakness
data.description:
• If you are wondering whether First Merchants at around US$36.55 is offering good value today, you are not alone. This article will walk through what the current price may be implying.
  • The stock has seen mixed returns, with a 5.2% decline over the last 7 days and a 12.5% decline over the last 30 days, while the 3 year return sits at 11.9% and the 1 year return at a 2.9% decline.
  • Recent trading moves have put more attention on how investors are pricing regional banks like First Merchants and what risks or opportunities might be getting more weight in the market. This context makes it a useful time to compare the market price with different ways of estimating what the shares could reasonably be worth.
  • On Simply Wall St's valuation checks, First Merchants scores 6 out of 6 for being assessed as undervalued. You can see that full breakdown in the valuation score of 6, which sets us up to look at several valuation approaches next and then finish with a broader way to think about what that valuation really means for you.

Find out why First Merchants's -2.9% return over the last year is lagging behind its peers.

Advertisement

Approach 1: First Merchants Excess Returns Analysis

The Excess Returns model looks at how much profit a company is expected to generate above the return that shareholders require, and then links that to the value of its equity. Instead of focusing on cash flows, it uses earnings, book value and the cost of equity to estimate what each share could be worth.

For First Merchants, the model starts with a Book Value of $42.87 per share and a Stable EPS of $4.52 per share, based on weighted future Return on Equity estimates from 5 analysts. The implied Cost of Equity is $3.26 per share, so the Excess Return is $1.27 per share. That excess is supported by an Average Return on Equity of 9.69% and a Stable Book Value estimate of $46.65 per share, sourced from 6 analysts.

When these inputs are run through the Excess Returns framework, Simply Wall St calculates an intrinsic value of about $82.12 per share. Compared with a current price around $36.55, this indicates the stock is 55.5% undervalued according to this model.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Our Excess Returns analysis suggests First Merchants is undervalued by 55.5%. Track this in your watchlist or portfolio, or discover 47 more high quality undervalued stocks.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for First Merchants.

Approach 2: First Merchants Price vs Earnings

For a profitable company like First Merchants, the P/E ratio is a useful way to think about value because it links what you are paying directly to the earnings the bank is currently generating. Investors typically accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future growth and how much risk they see in those earnings.

First Merchants is trading on a P/E of 10.34x. That sits below the Banks industry average P/E of 11.23x and below the peer group average of 13.40x. On the surface, that suggests the market is placing a lower multiple on First Merchants earnings than on many comparable banks.

Simply Wall St also uses a proprietary “Fair Ratio” to estimate what P/E might make sense for a company given factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and specific risks. This Fair Ratio, at 12.40x for First Merchants, goes beyond a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for the company’s own characteristics rather than assuming all banks deserve the same multiple. Comparing that Fair Ratio of 12.40x with the current P/E of 10.34x points to the shares trading below this model derived level.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Upgrade Your Decision Making: Choose your First Merchants Narrative

Earlier we mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation, so let us introduce you to Narratives, which simply means you write a clear story about First Merchants, link that story to specific forecasts for revenue, earnings and margins, then see what fair value falls out and compare it with today’s price.

On Simply Wall St’s Community page, Narratives are an easy tool that millions of investors use to capture their own view of a company and translate it into numbers. Instead of only looking at a P/E or analyst target, you directly connect your expectations for things like First Merchants revenue growth of 7.4% a year, a profit margin path from 35.0% to 28.1%, and a possible future P/E of 14.2x to an implied fair value that you can constantly compare with the live share price.

Because Narratives refresh when new earnings, news or risks emerge, one investor might see the analysts’ fair value of about US$46.83 as reasonable. Another might plug in more cautious assumptions about Midwest loan demand, funding mix or digital progress and land on a lower figure. That difference in fair values helps each of them decide for themselves whether the current price around US$40.22 looks attractive, neutral or expensive without anyone needing to predict outcomes for them.

data.title: Boeing’s Israel Bomb Deal Tests Defense Strength Versus 737 MAX Setbacks
data.description:
• Boeing (NYSE:BA) has secured a new $289 million contract to supply up to 5,000 precision guided bombs to Israel.
  • The deal arrives as Boeing faces fresh delivery delays tied to 737 MAX wiring issues in its commercial aircraft segment.
  • This order adds to Boeing's defense backlog at a time when investors are closely watching the split between its military and commercial businesses.

Boeing shares recently closed at $204.76, with the stock down 7.8% over the past week and 15.6% over the past month. Despite this pullback, the company shows a 28.5% return over the past year, while the 5 year return stands at a 20.0% decline, underscoring how mixed the long term picture has been for NYSE:BA.

The new precision bomb contract highlights how Boeing's defense unit can play a different role in a portfolio than its commercial aircraft business. The commercial segment is again under scrutiny due to 737 MAX delivery delays tied to wiring issues. For you as an investor, this raises an important question about how much weight to place on defense backlog growth versus ongoing quality and timing risks in the commercial segment.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Boeing by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Boeing.

4 things going right for Boeing that this headline doesn't cover.

This Israel precision-bomb contract gives Boeing’s defense business another long-duration revenue stream while its commercial segment works through fresh 737 MAX delivery delays tied to scratched wiring. The deal is relatively small compared with Boeing’s roughly US$85b defense backlog, but it adds volume in a product line where Boeing already sees sustained demand. Because deliveries are not expected for about three years, this is more of a backlog and capacity utilisation story than an immediate earnings driver, which matters if you are thinking about how predictable cash flows might look several years out.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Boeing Narrative

  • The contract aligns with the narrative that Boeing benefits from a diversified mix of commercial jets, services and defense programs. It provides another source of potential long-term earnings as air travel and fleet cycles evolve.
  • At the same time, ongoing 737 MAX wiring issues highlight execution and quality risks. The narrative already flags these as a headwind to margin recovery and confidence in Boeing’s commercial turnaround.
  • The contract is a direct commercial sale rather than a government-to-government deal. This adds nuance to Boeing’s defense exposure that may not be fully reflected in broad narrative themes about government-backed orders.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Boeing to help decide what it is worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Quality-control issues around 737 MAX wiring add to an existing pattern of operational questions that could affect delivery schedules and regulatory scrutiny.
  • ⚠️ Analysts have flagged 3 key risks for Boeing, including concerns that debt is not well covered by operating cash flow, which can limit flexibility if new issues arise in either commercial or defense programs.
  • 🎁 Boeing’s defense backlog of about US$85b, now including this Israel contract, provides multi-year revenue visibility that is less tied to airline cycles than its commercial business.
  • 🎁 The company is involved across commercial jets, defense systems and services, putting it alongside peers such as Airbus, Lockheed Martin and RTX in terms of having multiple potential earnings drivers.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you may want to track two things side by side: how quickly Boeing resolves the 737 MAX wiring rework without disrupting its delivery targets, and whether additional defense or services deals add to the already large backlog. Any updates on certification timelines for new 737 variants, or fresh regulatory commentary, will also feed into how durable the recovery case looks next to Boeing’s debt load and cash generation goals.

The Workato announcement sits alongside earlier AI ecosystem moves, such as the October 2025 launch of the DigitalOcean AI Partner Program with firms like Fal.ai and MongoDB. data.title: Corvus Trials Progress And Cash Raise Reframe Soquelitinib Risk Reward Profile
data.description:
• Corvus Pharmaceuticals has moved its lead candidate, soquelitinib, into late-stage Phase 3 and Phase 2 trials across several indications.
  • The company raised $189 million in a follow-on offering, which it says extends its cash runway into 2028.

Corvus Pharmaceuticals (NasdaqGM:CRVS) is drawing attention after pushing soquelitinib into more advanced clinical trials while securing fresh capital to support the work. The stock trades at $16.01, with very large 3-year and 1-year returns, and a more than 4x gain over 5 years, which reflects how much expectations have shifted around the story.

For investors, key factors to track now include clinical readouts from the new Phase 3 and expanded Phase 2 programs, and how management allocates the $189 million runway through 2028. These milestones, along with any updates around additional indications for soquelitinib, are likely to influence how the market views both the risk profile and the longer-term potential of NasdaqGM:CRVS.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Corvus Pharmaceuticals by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Corvus Pharmaceuticals.

See which insiders are buying and buying and selling Corvus Pharmaceuticals following this latest news.

For current shareholders, this update sits squarely at the intersection of funding, dilution and execution risk. The January follow-on offering that raised about US$189 million extended Corvus Pharmaceuticals’ cash runway into 2028. This means the company now has a clearer line of sight to key trial readouts without immediately returning to the market for more capital. At the same time, the raise has already contributed to substantial dilution over the past year, and the business still reports no revenue and a full year 2025 net loss of US$15.28 million, even though that loss was smaller than the prior year. The move of soquelitinib into a registrational Phase 3 trial and expanded Phase 2 work is what appears to be driving attention, but it also concentrates the story on a single clinical asset. Recent share price volatility, with sharp swings over short periods alongside very large multi year gains, suggests sentiment is tightly linked to trial and funding headlines. If you are following Corvus, this is the kind of event that can reset expectations around both risk tolerance and time horizon.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Corvus Pharmaceuticals Narrative

  • The new funding and late stage trials align with the narrative that multiple indications for soquelitinib create several shots on goal across immune and inflammatory diseases and T cell lymphomas.
  • The reliance on one mechanism, ITK inhibition, means any weak data from the Phase 3 peripheral T cell lymphoma or Phase 2 atopic dermatitis studies could challenge the idea that the platform will support a broad, multi indication pipeline.
  • The extended cash runway into 2028 is more recent than parts of the existing narrative, which referenced funding into late 2026, so some investors may not yet have reflected this longer funding window in their assumptions.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Corvus Pharmaceuticals to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Corvus remains a clinical stage company with no revenue and ongoing net losses, so the investment case still hinges on future trial outcomes converting into commercial products.
  • ⚠️ Shareholders have been substantially diluted over the past year and the stock price has been highly volatile over the past 3 months, which can make position sizing and timing more demanding for investors.
  • 🎁 Analysts have flagged that the shares trade well below one estimate of fair value, which some investors may view as a potential upside skew if clinical results are supportive.
  • 🎁 The expanded late stage program provides multiple upcoming data points that can act as catalysts, giving investors clearer information on whether the soquelitinib platform is progressing as planned.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, you will likely want to track three areas closely. First, the pace and quality of updates from the Phase 3 peripheral T cell lymphoma trial and the Phase 2 atopic dermatitis study, since these readouts sit at the core of the thesis. Second, management’s use of the US$189 million cash runway, especially how research and development spending is balanced between late stage work and earlier programs. Third, trading reactions around each clinical or funding update, as recent moves suggest sentiment can swing quickly. Competitive progress from other immunology and oncology players such as Regeneron, Sanofi or Incyte may also frame how differentiated investors see Corvus’ approach.

data.title: Strickland Metals H1 Loss Revives Doubts Around Earnings Quality And Bullish Profitability Narrative
data.description:
Strickland Metals (ASX:STK) has posted its H1 2026 numbers with revenue of A$2.1 million and a basic EPS loss of A$0.000892 per share, setting a cautious tone around the latest half. The company has seen revenue move from A$1.2 million in H1 2025 to A$7.4 million in H2 2025 and then A$2.1 million in H1 2026, while basic EPS has swung from a loss of A$0.0014 in H1 2025 to a profit of A$0.003091 in H2 2025 before slipping back to a small loss in the latest half, putting the spotlight firmly on how durable margins really are.

See our full analysis for Strickland Metals.

With the headline results on the table, the next step is to set these numbers against the prevailing narratives around growth, risks, and quality of earnings to see which stories hold up and which start to look stretched.

Curious how numbers become stories that shape markets? Explore Community Narratives

Advertisement

Trailing Profit Stays Positive at A$2.5 Million

  • Over the last 12 months, Strickland Metals reported net income of A$2.5 million on A$9.5 million of revenue, even though H1 2026 on its own showed a net loss of A$2.0 million.
  • What stands out is that earnings grew at about 20.5% per year over the past five years and the company only tipped into profitability over the last year. However, the latest half year loss means that recent profit progress and the move into the black are not on a straight line, which is something both cautious and optimistic investors will need to weigh against the longer trend.

Results like this often raise the question of how durable the story really is, and many investors turn to community views when the numbers send mixed signals. 📊 Read the what the Community is saying about Strickland Metals.

High P/B of 5.7x Sits Between Peers and Industry

  • The current P/B of 5.7x is below the 9.5x peer average but sits well above the broader Australian Metals & Mining sector at 2.4x. As a result, the shares trade cheaper than close comparables yet richer than the industry as a whole.
  • Critics highlight that this valuation sits alongside forecasts for earnings to fall about 52.7% per year and revenue to fall roughly 121.7% per year over the next three years. That combination of a higher sector multiple with forecast declines invites questions about how much of the recent A$2.5 million of trailing profit and 20.5% annual earnings growth is repeatable and how much simply reflects one good year.
    • Those bearish concerns also point to recent shareholder dilution as a further headwind for per share figures, given that more shares now sit against the same pool of earnings.
    • On the other hand, the fact that the trailing period moved from loss to profit while still trading below peer P/B leaves room for investors to debate whether peers or the wider sector are the better reference point for value.

Non Cash Earnings and Dilution Complicate the Story

  • Reported earnings over the last year contain a large non cash component, flagged as a high quality of earnings risk, and the trailing figures also include A$3.4 million of losses from discontinued operations, while shareholders saw dilution over the same period.
  • What is surprising is how strongly the recent move into profitability and five year earnings growth of 20.5% per year support a bullish story about operational progress. At the same time, the high proportion of non cash items, the A$3.4 million discontinued operations loss, and the fresh capital issuance all pull in the opposite direction by making it harder to read the A$2.5 million trailing profit as a clean signal on underlying cash generation per share.
    • Bulls may focus on the simple fact that the company is now profitable on a trailing basis, but the accounting mix means that cash returns to shareholders are not directly visible from these numbers alone.
    • Bears, in turn, can point to the combination of non cash earnings and dilution as reasons to treat reported EPS and book value metrics with extra care when deciding whether the current share price of A$0.21 offers enough compensation for those risks.

data.title: Moody’s Flags Hidden Private Credit Risks And Potential Business Implications
data.description:
• Moody's (NYSE:MCO) has issued a warning on the growing risks in complex structured private credit transactions.
  • The firm highlights hidden liabilities and unrecognized future obligations that may not appear in standard credit metrics.
  • Moody's is urging lenders, investors and insurers to adopt a more holistic approach to assessing credit risk across these structures.

Moody's, trading at $426.47, is calling attention to structured private credit deals that are increasingly used by investment grade companies and distributed into insurance portfolios. The stock shows mixed recent performance, with a 9.3% decline over the past week, a 1.8% return over 30 days and a 46.7% return over 3 years. This frames the warning against a backdrop of longer term shareholder gains and recent short term pressure.

For investors, the message is less about short term price moves and more about how credit risk is being packaged and held across the system. Moody's alert suggests market participants may want to scrutinize the structure of private credit exposures more closely, especially where headline credit metrics look comfortable but underlying obligations are complex or opaque.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Moody's by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Moody's.

We've flagged 2 risks for Moody's. See which could impact your investment.

For Moody's, this warning is not just a policy comment; it goes straight to the core of its business model as a risk assessor. As structured private credit becomes more widespread among investment grade borrowers and insurance balance sheets, investors and regulators are likely to look for tools that go beyond traditional leverage ratios and interest coverage. That plays directly into demand for more granular ratings, data and analytics that can unpack cash flow waterfalls, embedded options and off balance sheet commitments. At the same time, if hidden obligations lead to unexpected stress events, stakeholders may question how well rating frameworks kept up with market engineering, which could be a reputational and execution risk for Moody's and peers such as S&P Global and Fitch. The company is effectively signaling that it wants to be seen as early and vocal on these issues, which can help position its research and analytics as part of the solution, but it also raises the bar for how quickly its models and methodologies adapt.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Moody's Narrative

  • This focus on opaque private credit structures lines up with the narrative that growing private credit markets are a key driver of new rating mandates and demand for advanced analytics.
  • By highlighting potential underappreciated risks, Moody's is also pointing to regulatory and political scrutiny that the narrative already flags as a possible headwind for margins and growth in this segment.
  • The warning leans heavily on structural complexity and cash flow reshaping, which may not be fully captured in high level references to private credit growth and AI-powered analytics in the existing narrative.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Moody's to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ As complex private credit grows, any high profile failure that exposes hidden obligations could increase legal and regulatory pressure on rating agencies, with higher compliance costs for Moody's.
  • ⚠️ If market participants view rating opinions as slow to reflect structural risks in these deals, Moody's reputation as an independent risk arbiter could be questioned, especially against competitors like S&P Global and Fitch.
  • 🎁 Moody's early and detailed framing of the risks in structured private credit can support demand for its analytics, particularly from insurers and asset managers looking for deeper cash flow and covenant analysis.
  • 🎁 The trend toward more complex financing structures may increase the value of Moody's broader risk assessment toolkit across ratings, data and software, creating more ways to serve clients using its existing platforms.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it is worth tracking how often structured private credit risk appears in Moody's research, methodologies and conference commentary, including the upcoming BofA Securities Information & Business Services Conference. Pay attention to whether the firm rolls out new tools or scorecards that explicitly target private credit and insurance portfolios, and how quickly insurers and large sponsors adopt them. You can also watch for any regulatory statements that reference rating agency views on private credit, as that will influence the compliance and opportunity set for Moody's.

data.title: Chevron News Highlights Rising Geopolitical Risks And Portfolio Repositioning
data.description:
• Chevron declared force majeure at the Leviathan gas field in Israel, affecting Mediterranean gas supplies and regional energy security.
  • The company agreed to pay more than $1 million to settle a U.S. Department of Justice case related to renewable fuel credit compliance.
  • Chevron reached a deal to sell its operated interests in certain Angolan oil blocks to Energean.
  • The company is progressing petroleum agreements in Venezuela while geopolitical risks remain elevated.
  • These developments come after a share purchase by major investor Berkshire Hathaway.

For investors watching NYSE:CVX, these moves arrive while the stock trades at $196.97 and has returned 3.7% over the past week and 8.1% over the past month. Over longer periods, returns of 26.3% year to date and 34.0% over 1 year present Chevron as a company whose share price has been responsive to recent company and sector news.

What stands out now is how operational disruptions, legal settlements, and portfolio reshaping across Israel, the U.S., Angola, and Venezuela intersect for one of the largest listed energy companies. As you weigh Chevron’s mix of risks and opportunities, these developments highlight where its capital, regulatory attention, and geopolitical exposure are currently concentrated.

Stay updated on the most important news stories for Chevron by adding it to your watchlist or portfolio. Alternatively, explore our Community to discover new perspectives on Chevron.

📰 Beyond the headline: 2 risks and 2 things going right for Chevron that every investor should see.

The force majeure at Leviathan, the environmental compliance settlement, and the portfolio moves in Angola and Venezuela all point in the same direction for Chevron, which is to concentrate on assets and regions it wants to own for the long haul while accepting higher operational and regulatory complexity. Leviathan’s suspension shows how exposed Eastern Mediterranean gas is to security decisions, and it may temporarily affect gas-linked cash flows and contract relationships with buyers in Israel, Egypt, and Jordan. By contrast, selling offshore Angola interests to Energean and progressing Venezuelan agreements suggests Chevron is recycling capital from a mature African position into heavier crude and longer-life projects in the Orinoco Belt. The U.S. renewable fuel credit case, while small in dollar terms relative to Chevron’s size, is a reminder that compliance systems and environmental obligations are an ongoing cost of doing business, especially compared with peers like ExxonMobil and Shell. For you as an investor, the combined picture is a company leaning into complex, partnership-heavy projects while carrying both geopolitical and regulatory risk that needs to be weighed against its integrated scale.

Advertisement

How This Fits Into The Chevron Narrative

  • The push to deepen Venezuelan exposure, sell certain Angolan blocks, and keep large gas projects like Leviathan central to the portfolio ties in with the narrative that Chevron is leaning on long-lived, low-cost upstream positions and acquisitions to support future cash generation and dividend resilience.
  • Force majeure at Leviathan and the U.S. renewable fuel credit settlement highlight execution, regulatory, and geopolitical risks that sit alongside the narrative’s focus on growth from the Permian, Guyana, and Venezuela, and may challenge assumptions about smooth production and regulatory stability.
  • The specific compliance issues under the Renewable Fuel Standard and the near term cash flow and contract impact of Leviathan’s shutdown are not fully reflected in the broader narrative, even though both can influence how investors think about future margins and risk-adjusted returns across Chevron’s portfolio.

Knowing what a company is worth starts with understanding its story. Check out one of the top narratives in the Simply Wall St Community for Chevron to help decide what it's worth to you.

The Risks and Rewards Investors Should Consider

  • ⚠️ Greater exposure to high risk jurisdictions such as Venezuela, along with conflict affected areas like Israel and wider Middle East routes, could lead to more frequent production interruptions, contract renegotiations, or changes to fiscal terms.
  • ⚠️ The environmental compliance settlement under the U.S. Renewable Fuel Standard underscores ongoing regulatory and ESG scrutiny that can add to costs and may feed into concerns analysts already flag about dividend coverage and insider selling.
  • 🎁 Recycling capital from offshore Angola into higher impact projects in Venezuela and elsewhere can sharpen Chevron’s focus on assets it views as core to long term cash generation compared with global peers such as ExxonMobil and BP.
  • 🎁 Maintaining a broad, integrated portfolio across oil, gas, and LNG, even with local disruptions at Leviathan, gives Chevron multiple cash flow sources, which can help it manage through regional shocks while it pursues longer-dated projects.

What To Watch Going Forward

From here, it makes sense to watch how long Leviathan production remains constrained and whether there are any changes to sales contracts or expansion timelines for that field. You may also want to track progress on Venezuelan agreements, including any details on royalties, taxes, and export conditions, and how quickly Chevron closes the Angolan divestment and redeploys that capital. Finally, keep an eye on further regulatory disclosures around renewable fuels and broader ESG matters, especially if you are already following existing analyst concerns about dividend coverage and insider transactions.

Approach 1: Mattel Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company may be worth by projecting the cash it could generate in the future and then discounting those cash flows back to today using a required return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in current terms.

For Mattel, the model used here is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach. The company’s last twelve months free cash flow is about $413.0 million. Analysts provide explicit free cash flow estimates out to 2028, where projected free cash flow is $435.65 million, and the remaining years out to 2035 are extrapolated based on those earlier expectations.

When all these projected cash flows are discounted back and combined, the model arrives at an estimated intrinsic value of about $28.78 per share. Against the recent share price of $16.22, this implies an intrinsic discount of roughly 43.6%, which means the stock screens as undervalued on this DCF view.

Result: UNDERVALUED

Approach 2: Mattel Price vs Earnings

For a profitable business like Mattel, the P/E ratio is a useful way to look at value because it directly links what you pay for each share to the company’s current earnings. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how risky they think those earnings are.

Mattel’s current P/E is 12.33x. That sits below both the Leisure industry average of 21.05x and the peer group average of 43.01x, which suggests the market is pricing Mattel’s earnings more cautiously than many of its peers.
